champion.narrative: Champion status is developing. Daehwan Kim (Purchasing Manager, Evaluator role) is the sole engaged buyer contact and shows genuine interest: he initiated the conversation (Prashant's Feb call, Daehwan asked to reconnect in June), articulated operational pain on the 03 Jun 2026 discovery call, and requested specific proof points (real-life ROI examples, demos, tailored use case). The BD team debrief post-call described him as 'an evaluator and champion here if we can sort of communicate with him in the right way,' and noted he is 'excited and quite optimistic about AI and automation' and that 'it's his job to evaluate softwares and sort of present them to his hierarchy.' However, champion strength is limited: (1) He has not introduced Zycus to the Economic Buyer, Decision Maker, or any senior stakeholder (CFO, VP Finance, Sr Dir Sourcing). (2) He ended the 03 Jun call abruptly, frustrated by high-level explanations, signaling Zycus has not yet demonstrated value in the way he needs. (3) No evidence of active advocacy (internal selling, pushing back on status quo, driving the agenda). (4) Engagement is passive since 03 Jun—rep-initiated Showpad outreach 22 Jun awaits his reply; no proactive follow-up from the relevant stakeholder has developed: curiosity, articulated pain, willingness to review materials. What is missing: access to power, proof of internal selling, proactive next-step ownership, multi-threading. Current engagement: cooling (no two-way touch in 28 days). The critical move is to re-engage Daehwan with the promised Showpad proof points, convert interest to advocacy, and secure an introduction to the Economic Buyer or senior procurement leader.

competition.narrative: Competition is the status quo (manual processes, no S2P platform in place). Daehwan Kim confirmed on the 03 Jun 2026 discovery call that Nellson relies on manual procurement: category managers handle supplier onboarding and contract follow-up, JD Edwards MRP flags orders but execution is manual, and there is no contract lifecycle management system or automated sourcing tool. He stated they have 'JD Edwards, but no automation,' and described the manual process as a company weakness. No mention of evaluating other S2P vendors (Coupa, SAP Ariba, Ivalua, GEP, Zip, Jaggaer, etc.) on the call or in Salesforce fields. The Competitors__c field is unpopulated. The wedge is automation and AI-native capabilities (Merlin intake, autonomous negotiation agents, single-code architecture, contract utilization visibility) against a manual baseline. However, the risk is status-quo inertia: without a compelling event, quantified ROI, or executive sponsorship, the deal may stall at 'interesting but not urgent.' Daehwan's frustration with high-level explanations ('you are providing me that very, very, like, generic information only') signals Zycus has not yet proven differentiation in a way that compels action. The competitive threat is do-nothing, not a rival vendor. How we win: deliver the practical proof points Daehwan requested (real-life ROI examples, recorded demos, tailored use case with measurable before/after metrics), quantify the cost of manual processes, and connect to the Economic Buyer to create urgency.

identify_pain.narrative: Pain is confirmed and articulated by Daehwan Kim (Purchasing Manager) on the 03 Jun 2026 discovery call. Three operational pain points were named: (1) Manual procurement execution causing human error—JD Edwards MRP flags orders, but category managers execute manually, leading to mistakes in ordering and demand review. (2) Overwhelmed category managers handling supplier onboarding and contract follow-up manually, identified as a company weakness. (3) No visibility into contract utilization or renewals—contracts sit unmanaged, and the team reviews them manually only when needed, causing risk of running out volume mid-contract or missing renewal windows. Daehwan stated, 'the problem I always see is that there's a lot of human error could happen. So I was very interested about any kind of automation,' and described contract management as 'one of the our company's weakness. That nobody was really following up the contract.' The pain owner is Daehwan's purchasing team (category managers), but the financial/executive pain owner (who feels the P&L impact) is unmapped—likely the CFO or VP Finance. Pain is articulated but not quantified; no baseline cost, error rate, or FTE hours stated.

decision_criteria.narrative: Decision criteria partially articulated on the 03 Jun 2026 discovery call. Daehwan Kim confirmed interest in three workflow areas: sourcing (RFX/RFP automation), contract lifecycle management (visibility into contract utilization, renewals, alerts), and supplier onboarding/management (automate manual category-manager-led process). He also emphasized JD Edwards MRP integration to ingest ERP data and reduce ordering errors. A critical criterion surfaced during the call: Daehwan repeatedly asked for 'real-life examples' and 'demonstrable before/after metrics'—he wants proof the platform works in practice, not generic capability overviews. He stated, 'I care that how this module can actually affect my real life,' and ended the call frustrated when high-level explanations did not satisfy that need. The implied decision criterion is practical proof of value: customer case studies with hard ROI numbers, recorded demos showing real workflows, and a tailored use case illustrating savings/cycle-time improvements for Nellson's manual procurement baseline. No formal RFP, RFI, or scoring rubric has been mentioned; criteria are still informal and discovery-phase.

ai_positioning_strength.summary: AI positioning is under-sold. Daehwan Kim stated he is 'excited and quite optimistic about AI and automation' (BD team debrief 03 Jun) and requested automation to reduce manual process and human error. Zycus presented AI-native capabilities on the 03 Jun call (Merlin intake, autonomous negotiation agents, single-code AI across modules, 32 patents, Gartner MQ leadership), but Daehwan's frustration with 'generic information' and his request for 'real-life examples' of how AI works in practice signals the AI story did not land. The platform's AI strengths (Merlin intake via Teams, autonomous agents for tail-spend negotiation, contract risk/redlining AI, supplier-risk monitoring) were described but not demonstrated or anchored to Nellson's manual baseline. The buyer is AI-curious; the positioning is capability-led, not outcome-led. To strengthen: in Showpad materials and solution consultant demo, lead with AI-driven outcomes tied to Nellson's pain—e.g. 'Merlin intake in Teams reduces requisition creation time from 15 min to 2 min, cutting FTE hours 80%' or 'AI contract alerts caught $2M in unutilized volume for, preventing overbuying'—and show live AI workflows, not feature lists.

customer_expectations_fit.summary: Nellson's articulated needs align well with Zycus capabilities: JD Edwards MRP integration (confirmed capability), contract lifecycle management with utilization tracking (iContract), automated supplier onboarding (iSupplier), and sourcing/RFX automation (iSource). The critical misalignment is proof-of-value delivery: Daehwan repeatedly asked for 'real-life examples' and 'demonstrable before/after metrics' but received high-level platform overviews, causing frustration. He ended the 03 Jun call early, stating, 'you are providing me that very, very, like, generic information only, but that does not really helping to understand that this system really can help us.' Zycus committed to Showpad package with demos and tailored use case to address this, but delivery is pending (rep outreach 22 Jun, awaiting buyer engagement). value.name: Status quo / do nothing (manual processes, no S2P platform)
value.quote: Daehwan Kim confirmed they have JD Edwards MRP but no automation; everything is manual (supplier onboarding, contract management, procurement execution). He stated, 'there is a lot of human error could happen. So I was very interested about any kind of automation,' and described contract management as 'one of the our company's weakness.'
value.source: Avoma discovery call 03 Jun 2026
value.how_we_win: Quantify the cost of manual processes (error rate, FTE hours, contract leakage), deliver practical proof points Daehwan requested (real-life ROI examples with hard numbers, recorded demos, tailored use case showing before/after metrics), and connect to Economic Buyer (CFO or VP Finance) to create urgency and secure budget approval. Emphasize AI-native automation (Merlin intake, autonomous negotiation agents, contract utilization visibility) as the wedge against manual baseline.

value.name: Status quo / do nothing (manual processes, no S2P platform)
value.quote: Procurement is manual across supplier management, demand review, contracts, and purchasing. JD Edwards is in place, but automation is limited. Human error is a key concern. Contract utilization and follow-ups are not proactively tracked.
value.source: SF Customer_Business_Problem__c and Task 03 Jun (Avoma discovery notes)
value.how_we_win: Quantify the annual cost of manual errors, contract misses, and inefficient supplier onboarding (cycle time, headcount, rework). Present that figure as the 'price' of staying with the status quo. Position Zycus AI/automation (Merlin intake, CLM with utilization tracking, autonomous negotiation) as the forcing function to eliminate that cost, with concrete ROI examples from similar food/FMCG manufacturers. Make the business case so compelling that deferring the decision becomes more expensive than acting.
